title: Marble loaf cake
subtitle: The vanilla and chocolate swirl loaf, reliable and handsome
excerpt: A 900 g marble loaf with a vanilla batter divided in two: half stays plain, half gets cocoa. The two are spooned alternately into the tin and swirled once with a skewer. The swirl reveals itself on the cut face. Serves 10.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the marbled cake section describes a pound cake batter divided and coloured, layered alternately in a loaf tin. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton documents the technique of coloured batter layering and the folding method for maintaining the marble pattern. The specific vanilla-and-cocoa combination became the British standard from the Victorian era onward as cocoa became widely available; the swirling approach is consistent across the domestic baking literature of the period.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 loaf (900 g / 2 lb tin, cuts to 10 slices)
recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze in a bag for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bakes up to 2 days ahead; keeps well wrapped at room temperature. The marble pattern is clearer on day one.

recipeTools.notes: 900 g (2 lb) tin, greased and lined.

glossaryTerms.definition: Running a skewer or thin knife once through alternating spoonfuls of two-coloured batters to create a swirled pattern in the baked cake. One swirl : a figure-of-eight or an S-shape through the length of the tin is enough. More swirling muddies the two colours into grey.

content.text: The marble loaf is a single batter divided in two: vanilla goes into the tin as it is, and cocoa goes into the other half. They're spooned in alternating dollops, and then a skewer draws a single S-shaped path through the tin to connect the swirls. The pattern looks more involved than it is; the restraint of one swirl is what keeps the marble distinct rather than blending into a uniform beige.

items.prepNote: extra milk for the chocolate half, to compensate for the drying effect of cocoa

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease and line a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter and {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar until pale and fluffy (about 4 minutes). Add the {{eggs}} eggs one at a time, beating well after each. Add the {{vanilla-extract}}. Fold in the {{self-raising-flour}} sifted self-raising flour in two stages. Stir in enough {{whole-milk}} milk to give a batter that falls from a spoon in a slow ribbon — about 60 ml. Divide the batter equally between two bowls.

Sift the {{cocoa-powder}} into one bowl of batter and add the extra 30 ml of milk. Fold until fully combined and uniformly dark — the cocoa absorbs moisture and makes the batter slightly stiffer than the plain half; the extra milk compensates.
Spoon the batters alternately into the prepared tin — two tablespoons of vanilla, two of chocolate, repeat until both batters are used. Make a
by running a skewer once through the length of the tin in an S-shape, going from one end to the other and from the surface to the base of the batter. One pass only.

Bake for 50–55 minutes, until the top is golden-brown, a skewer through the centre comes out clean, and the loaf has pulled away slightly from the tin sides. Leave in the tin for 15 minutes, then transfer to a rack. The marble pattern is clearest when the loaf is sliced cold.
